one_liner: Ordered, deduplicated pub/sub messaging that fans out to multiple SQS FIFO queues or other subscribers with strict message ordering.
when_to_use: Need ordered, exactly-once message fanout to multiple consumers
Event-driven architectures requiring strict ordering guarantees across multiple subscribers
Financial transactions, inventory updates, or other workflows where message order matters
Replacing point-to-point ordered queues with ordered pub/sub patterns
when_not_to_use: High-throughput scenarios exceeding 300 messages/second per message group (use standard SNS + application-level ordering)
Subscribers that do not need ordering guarantees (use standard SNS topics for higher throughput)
Streaming data ingestion (use Kinesis Data Streams)
key_facts: SNS FIFO topics deliver messages in strict order within a message group, with exactly-once processing via deduplication.
Can fan out directly to multiple SQS FIFO queue subscriptions. You do NOT need a single SQS queue to receive and re-distribute.
Supports up to 300 messages/second (or 10 MB/second) per FIFO topic, with up to 3,000 messages/second with batching.
Message groups allow parallel processing of independent message streams while maintaining order within each group.
Content-based deduplication or explicit deduplication IDs prevent duplicate delivery.
Currently supports SQS FIFO queues as subscribers. Does not support Lambda, HTTP, or email subscriptions directly (route through SQS FIFO to Lambda).
Topic name must end with .fifo suffix.
pricing_summary: SNS FIFO: $0.50 per million published messages + $0.06 per million subscription deliveries. SQS FIFO subscribers: standard SQS FIFO pricing applies.
common_misconceptions: SNS FIFO CAN fan out to multiple subscribers directly. You do NOT need a single SQS FIFO queue with Lambda to re-distribute messages to other queues.
SNS FIFO ordering is per message group, NOT per topic. Different message groups can be processed in parallel.
SNS FIFO does NOT support all subscription protocols. Currently limited to SQS FIFO queues as subscribers.
FIFO topics and Standard topics are different resources. You cannot convert between them after creation.

one_liner: Fully managed message queuing service with Standard (at-least-once, best-effort ordering) and FIFO (exactly-once, strict ordering) queue types for decoupling distributed systems.
when_to_use: Decouple producers from consumers to handle traffic spikes without data loss
Buffer writes to a database or downstream service that cannot handle peak throughput
Work queue pattern: distribute tasks across a fleet of workers with Auto Scaling based on queue depth
Ensure reliable message delivery with automatic retries and dead-letter queues for failed processing
Order processing, job queuing, or any asynchronous workflow needing durable message storage
when_not_to_use: Fan-out to multiple subscribers (use SNS for pub/sub, then subscribe SQS queues to SNS topic)
Real-time streaming with replay capability (use Kinesis Data Streams)
Event routing with content-based filtering across many targets (use EventBridge)
key_facts: Standard queues: at-least-once delivery, best-effort ordering, nearly unlimited throughput. Messages may be delivered more than once or out of order.

FIFO queues: exactly-once processing, strict ordering within message groups. Throughput: 300 messages/second (3,000 with batching) per queue without high-throughput mode. High-throughput FIFO mode supports up to 70,000 messages/second.
FIFO queue names must end with .fifo suffix.
Message retention: 1 minute to 14 days (default 4 days).
Maximum message size: 256 KB. For larger payloads, use SQS Extended Client Library to store payload in S3.

Visibility timeout: when a consumer receives a message, it becomes invisible to other consumers for a configurable period (default 30 seconds). If not deleted within that period, it becomes visible again for reprocessing.
Dead-letter queues (DLQ): messages that fail processing after maxReceiveCount attempts are automatically moved to a DLQ for investigation.
Long polling (WaitTimeSeconds > 0): reduces empty responses and API costs by waiting for messages to arrive. Always prefer long polling over short polling.
Lambda integration: SQS can trigger Lambda functions via event source mappings. Lambda automatically handles polling, batching, and scaling.
Auto Scaling integration: use ApproximateNumberOfMessagesVisible metric to scale EC2 worker fleets based on queue depth.
Server-side encryption: supports SSE-SQS (AWS-managed) or SSE-KMS (customer-managed keys).
SNS-to-SQS fan-out: subscribe multiple SQS queues to a single SNS topic for parallel processing by different consumers.
pricing_summary: Standard: $0.40 per million requests. FIFO: $0.50 per million requests. First 1M requests/month free. Requests include Send, Receive, Delete, and other API actions. Each 64 KB chunk counts as one request.
common_misconceptions: SQS Standard does NOT guarantee message ordering. If ordering matters, use FIFO queues with message group IDs.
SQS is NOT pub/sub. It is point-to-point (one consumer per message). For fan-out, put SNS in front of multiple SQS queues.
SQS messages are NOT automatically deleted after reading. The consumer MUST explicitly delete the message after successful processing, or it will reappear after the visibility timeout.
DLQ is NOT automatic. You must explicitly configure a redrive policy with maxReceiveCount on the source queue.
FIFO ordering is per message group, NOT per queue. Different message group IDs can be processed in parallel while maintaining order within each group.
SQS-triggered Lambda scales automatically. Each batch of messages is processed by a separate Lambda invocation, and Lambda manages polling concurrency.

one_liner: Serverless event bus that routes events from AWS services, SaaS applications, and custom sources to targets using content-based filtering rules.
when_to_use: React to AWS service events (EC2 state changes, S3 events, API calls via CloudTrail, CodePipeline status changes)
Build event-driven architectures with decoupled producers and consumers
Route events from SaaS partners (Zendesk, Datadog, Auth0, Shopify) into AWS workflows
Content-based filtering: route events to different targets based on event attributes without custom code
Schedule-based automation (cron or rate expressions) as a replacement for CloudWatch Events scheduled rules
when_not_to_use: High-throughput streaming data ingestion (use Kinesis Data Streams)
Simple message queuing with guaranteed ordering (use SQS FIFO)
Point-to-point message delivery with visibility timeout (use SQS)
key_facts: EventBridge is the evolution of CloudWatch Events. Same underlying service with expanded features (schema registry, SaaS integrations, custom event buses).
Event buses: default bus (AWS service events), custom buses (application events), and partner buses (SaaS integrations).
Rules: match events using event patterns (JSON-based filter on event attributes) and route to one or more targets.
Targets include: Lambda, SQS, SNS, Step Functions, Kinesis, CodePipeline, ECS tasks, API Gateway, and more (over 20 target types).
Content-based filtering: rules can filter on any field in the event JSON, including nested fields, numeric matching, prefix/suffix matching, and exists checks.
Schema Registry: automatically discovers and stores event schemas. Generates code bindings for Java, Python, and TypeScript.
Archive and Replay: archive events and replay them for debugging, testing, or reprocessing failed events.
Scheduler: fully managed scheduling with cron and rate expressions, one-time schedules, and flexible time windows. Replaces CloudWatch Events scheduling.
Cross-account and cross-region event delivery: send events between AWS accounts and regions for centralized event processing.
Guaranteed at-least-once event delivery. Events are durably stored before delivery to targets.
Pipes: point-to-point integrations from source to target with optional filtering and enrichment (e.g., SQS to Step Functions with Lambda enrichment).
pricing_summary: Custom events: $1.00 per million events published. AWS service events: free on default event bus. Schema Registry: free discovery, $0.10 per million events ingested. Archive: $0.10/GB stored. Scheduler: free for most use cases.
common_misconceptions: EventBridge IS CloudWatch Events (renamed and expanded). Existing CloudWatch Events rules continue to work on the default EventBridge bus.
EventBridge is NOT a message queue. It is an event router. Events are delivered to targets and not stored for consumers to poll (unlike SQS).
EventBridge rules can match on ANY field in the event JSON, not just event source and detail-type. Content-based filtering is very powerful.
AWS service events (EC2 state changes, etc.) are delivered to the default bus for FREE. You only pay for custom events you publish.
